does: This paper systematically diagnoses why large language model judges exhibit strong inter-LLM agreement while diverging from human judgments. Using four geometric measures—score spread, effective rank, principal angle to human evaluation subspace, and stacked correlations—the authors analyze 41 LLM judges across 8 Indic languages and 4 community-built datasets. They find that on subjective rubrics, LLM judges use only 30-50% of the human score range, their evaluation axis is nearly orthogonal to human axes (87-89° versus 78-81° between humans), and inter-LLM agreement substantially exceeds LLM-human agreement (r≈0.35 vs r≈0.27-0.32). Critically, when rubrics have verifiable factual answers, geometric properties return to human-like ranges, suggesting LLMs converge on systematic biases rather than capturing human values on subjective tasks. Fine-tuning and preference optimization partially recover the full score spread.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using language models as automated evaluators, which has become standard in NLP evaluation despite limited validation against human judgment. It extends prior work examining LLM alignment and value learning by adopting geometric analysis tools—principal angles and correlation matrices—previously used in representation learning and neural network interpretability. The paper responds to assumptions underlying recent evaluation practices that treat LLM consensus as a proxy for quality, and connects to broader concerns about whether language models genuinely capture human preferences or merely exhibit consistent systematic biases. It leverages multilingual and multi-dataset evaluation frameworks from recent work on cross-lingual NLP, while invoking preference optimization methods like those used in RLHF and DPO to test whether fine-tuning can correct judge behavior.
enables: This work opens several directions for improving LLM-based evaluation. It demonstrates that geometric diagnostics can reliably detect when LLM judges diverge from human values, enabling researchers to identify problematic evaluation settings before deployment. The finding that preference optimization recovers human-like score spreads suggests pathways to align judges through targeted training rather than accepting their default behavior. The paper points toward auditing protocols for evaluating new judge configurations across rubric types and languages. It also raises questions about what LLM judges actually optimize for on subjective tasks—whether existing architectural or training choices systematically bias them away from human judgment axes. Future work could explore whether this divergence extends beyond Indic languages, investigate what linguistic or semantic properties trigger orthogonal evaluation axes, and develop better alignment methods that preserve both human-like spread and human-centered evaluation criteria.
